text: Short-term training cessation (less than 30 days) causes a significant moderate decline in VO2max with an effect size of -0.62.
Long-term training cessation (30+ days) causes a significant large decline in VO2max with an effect size of -1.42, more than double the short-term effect.
The VO2max decline from long-term detraining is significantly greater than from short-term detraining, confirming that longer breaks cause disproportionately more fitness loss.

There is no significant additional VO2max decline beyond 30 days of detraining — the difference between 30-90 days and greater than 90 days of cessation is not statistically significant, suggesting most VO2max loss occurs in the first 30 days.
Younger individuals (under 20) show a greater reduction in VO2max after long-term detraining compared to adults, suggesting youth athletes lose cardiorespiratory fitness faster during breaks.
Athletes with higher baseline VO2max experience a significantly greater absolute decline in VO2max during long-term detraining compared to those with lower baseline fitness.
population: highly trained athletes vs lower-trained athletes

text: Performing some physical exercise during training cessation can effectively reduce the detrimental effects of detraining on VO2max, particularly during long-term breaks.
